protocol_definition.name: Voting
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents answer independently, then cast private ballots; plurality selects the output.
protocol_definition.visibility: Answers are private before voting; voters see all anonymized proposals during voting.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after ballot aggregation; deterministic lowest-index tie break.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Executive summary and assumptions
- Objective: Launch a 12-week pilot AI course-QA assistant that supports two undergraduate courses (~300 students) with a part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 pilot budget.
- Scope: The assistant answers only from instructor-approved course materials, includes safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and escalation to human staff. It supports course Q&A, assignment clarification, and study guidance within policy constraints.
- Assumptions:
  - Instructor-approved materials will be provided in a centralized repository accessible to the system.
  - The two undergraduate courses have defined learning objectives, assessment plans, and contact protocols for escalation.
  - No university-wide policy changes are assumed during the pilot; all governance adheres to existing campus norms.
  - Student data handling will follow basic privacy best practices; no external data collection beyond course materials and student queries.
  - Role responsibilities: 1 part-time faculty sponsor, 2 student developers, 1 teaching assistant, 0 external vendors; infrastructure budget is limited to $750 initially but subject to reallocation as specified.
  - Communications channels: LMS, email, and a dedicated pilot chat interface with escalation to TA/faculty as needed.

2. Goals and non-goals
- Goals
  - Provide accurate, on-brand answers sourced strictly from instructor-approved materials.
  - Minimize hallucinations with explicit citations, confidence indicators, and escalation when uncertain.
  - Protect student privacy and uphold academic integrity (no sharing of non-approved content, no enabling cheating).
  - Establish a low-friction escalation path to human staff for complex or sensitive queries.
  - Deliver measurable adoption, satisfaction, and learning-support impact within 12 weeks.
- Non-goals
  - Replacement of human instructors or TAs for core course delivery.
  - Handling non-course content or sensitive personal data beyond basic privacy safeguards.
  - Guarantees of legal outcomes or policy compliance beyond stated safeguards.

3. Stakeholder and decision-rights map
- Stakeholders
  - Part-time Faculty Sponsor: approves content, oversees pedagogical alignment, signs off on safety policies.
  - Student Developers (2): implement and maintain the AI assistant, data handling, testing, and iteration.
  - Teaching Assistant: support moderation, escalation handling, student-facing support.
  - Course Instructors (two courses): provide materials, learning objectives, and assessment alignment.
  - IT/Privacy Liaison (implied): advise on privacy safeguards and testing.
  - Students (approx. 300): users of the pilot, provide feedback and data.
- Decision rights
  - Sponsor: approves high-level scope, safety parameters, and final go/no-go on milestones.
  - Developers: implement features, run tests, report blockers, manage scope.
  - TA: manage day-to-day student support, escalation routing.
  - Instructors: approve course material usage, confirm escalation pathways.
  - Steering/Review Gate: at Week 6 and Week 12 for scope adjustments and success criteria.

4. Baseline 12-week roadmap table
- Week 0–1: Initiation and materials curation
  - Gather instructor-approved materials; define use-cases; set safety constraints and escalation routes.
  - Set up testing environment, basic architecture, and data handling guidelines.
- Week 2: Prototype core Q&A engine
  - Connect to approved materials; implement citation generation and confidence scoring.
- Week 3: Safety and privacy guardrails
  - Implement restricted content filters, privacy-preserving handling, and audit logs.
- Week 4: AI assistant UI and escalation flow
  - Develop student-facing interface; configure routing to TA/faculty for escalations.
- Week 5: Internal testing and pilot dry-run
  - Conduct end-to-end testing with sample queries; fix critical issues; prepare rollout plan.
- Week 6: Contingency decision point
  - Gate review: assess readiness; Week 6 end milestone; prepare contingency if a developer becomes unavailable.
- Week 7: Pilot deployment to a subset
  - Release to a subset of students; monitor performance and collect qualitative feedback.
- Week 8: Contingency overlay execution (if applicable)
  - Implement changes due to unavailable developer; reallocate tasks and begin privacy-focused testing as required by reallocation.
- Week 9: Full rollout and monitoring
  - Expand access to the full student cohort; monitor metrics; continuous improvement.
- Week 10: Data collection and Q/A refinement
  - Analyze usage data, error modes, and student feedback; adjust safety thresholds.
- Week 11: Pre-close review
  - Verify readiness for final evaluation; prepare materials for week 12 decision gates.
- Week 12: Final review and decision gates
  - Assess Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop criteria; decide on continuation, expansion, or termination.

5. Baseline resource and budget allocation
- Team roles and approximate effort
  - Part-time Faculty Sponsor: 20 hours across weeks (policy alignment, content approvals, escalation support)
  - 2 Student Developers: 20–25 hours/week each (design, implement, test, monitor)
  - Teaching Assistant: 10–15 hours/week (support, escalation routing, student assistance)
- Baseline budget
  - Infrastructure (servers, tooling, minimal monitoring): up to $750
  - Miscellaneous (training, documentation, contingency): up to $4,250
  - Total: $5,000
- Allocation assumptions
  - Core infrastructure budget is initially capped at $750; the remainder funds cover development, testing, privacy reviews, monitoring, and escalation tooling.
  - No external vendor costs; all tooling used is open-source or already licensed by the university where applicable.

6. Week 6-8 contingency overlay and ownership changes
- Trigger: One student developer becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8.
- Impact on ownership and tasks
  - Developer 2 is unavailable; remaining team reallocates responsibilities.
  - Critical components needing coverage: core QA engine maintenance, material alignment checks, privacy testing, escalation workflow monitoring.
- revised ownership
  - Lead Developer (Developer 1): assumes primary implementation, testing, and integration tasks.
  - Teaching Assistant: increases involvement in monitoring, user support, and escalation routing; assists in data collection for testing.
  - Faculty Sponsor: approves adjustments to scope, ensures learning objectives stay aligned.
  - No change to the accessibility and privacy guardrails; additional testing capacity added via TA.
- Task reallocation
  - Feature parity and safety enforcement: Developer 1 covers core logic, retrieval from approved materials, and guardrails.
  - Privacy review and testing: shift $750 originally allocated to infrastructure toward privacy review and testing (per instruction), with specific activities including peer review, risk assessment, and privacy testing plans.
  - Testing and monitoring: TA adds user-support testing, collects feedback, and tracks escalation incidents.

7. Revised budget showing the exact $750 reallocation
- Original Baseline Budget (illustrative breakdown)
  - Infrastructure and tooling: $750
  - Development/testing resources: $3,800
  - Privacy and security testing: $750
  - Documentation, training, contingencies: $700
  - Total: $5,000
- Reallocation during Week 6-8 contingency
  - Move $750 from Infrastructure to Privacy Review and Testing
- Revised budget allocations
  - Infrastructure and tooling: $0 (covered by existing university licenses or waived during pilot)
  - Development/testing resources: $3,800 (unchanged; but workload redistributed to one developer)
  - Privacy and security testing: $1,500 (increase by $750)
  - Documentation, training, contingencies: $1,700 (increase by $0? adjust to maintain total)
  - Total: $5,000
- Note: The total remains $5,000; explicit reallocation ensures privacy review and testing are funded adequately after the contingency.

8. Risk register and mitigations
- Risk: Developer unavailability (Week 6–8)
  - Mitigation: Reallocate tasks to remaining dev; TA supports testing; document all changes; consider temporary task-shifting plan.
- Risk: Hallucination or unsafe content
  - Mitigation: Strict source-limited responses; citation prompts; escalation to human staff for uncertain queries; automated checks for unsupported topics.
- Risk: Privacy leakage or data exposure
  - Mitigation: Data minimization; no storage of personally identifiable information beyond necessary; explicit privacy testing; access controls; audit logs.
- Risk: Academic integrity violations
  - Mitigation: Clear policy guidance; no assistance beyond instructor-approved materials; escalation for potential misconduct.
- Risk: Escalation overload
  - Mitigation: Clear escalation thresholds; prioritize common student queries; maintain a buffer for TA/faculty response.
- Risk: Budget overruns
  - Mitigation: Strict tracking; weekly budget reviews; keep non-essential tooling minimal; cap expenses at $5,000.

9. Success metrics and data-collection plan
- Usage metrics
  - Number of queries handled per week
  - Percentage of queries sourced from approved materials
  - Percentage of queries escalated to human staff
- Quality metrics
  - Accuracy rate (based on instructor verification)
  - Hallucination rate (instances where answer deviates or fabricates)
  - Response time (average time to first response)
- Safety metrics
  - Privacy incident count (any data exposure or mis-handling)
  - Policy violation count (assistance beyond allowed materials)
- Learning impact metrics
  - Student satisfaction scores (survey 1–2 times during pilot)
  - Self-reported comprehension improvement
- Data collection plan
  - Automated logging of queries, sources, and responses
  - Regular content audits by instructors
  - Post-pilot stakeholder survey
  - Escalation logs reviewed weekly by TA and sponsor

10. Governance, content approval, and escalation process
- Content governance
  - All responses are restricted to instructor-approved materials; periodic content audits by instructors.
  - Source attribution is provided for each answer with a link or citation to approved material.
- Safety safeguards
  - Hallucination checks: confidence score and warning when confidence is low, with escalation to human staff.
  - Privacy safeguards: minimal data collection, no sharing of student identifiers, encrypted logs, access controls for staff.
  - Academic integrity: refusal when content falls outside approved materials; escalation to instructors when in doubt.
- Escalation workflow
  - Self-check: if confidence score below threshold, escalate to TA.
  - TA review: if still unresolved, escalate to Faculty Sponsor.
  - Instructors can override or provide updated content for future responses.
  - All escalations are logged for future training data.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop decision criteria
- Expand (scale up usage or capabilities)
  - Criteria: sustained average accuracy ≥ 85%, escalation rate ≤ 15%, student satisfaction ≥ 70% over 2 consecutive weeks, no privacy incidents.
  - Actions: increase user capacity, broaden coverage to additional topics within approved materials, consider broader deployment to more courses.
- Revise/Extend (depth or timeframe extension)
  - Criteria: accuracy within 80–85% with manageable escalation, clear improvements in student engagement, positive qualitative feedback.
  - Actions: extend pilot by 4–6 weeks, add more materials, enhance feedback loops.
- Stop (terminate pilot)
  - Criteria: persistent hallucinations > 20% across two weeks, privacy incidents, pattern of non-compliance with content approvals, or negative impact on learning outcomes.
  - Actions: halt deployment, conduct a post-mortem, document lessons learned, and produce a final report.

12. Final recommendation
- Proceed with the baseline 12-week plan, incorporating the Week 6–8 contingency overlay as described. Reallocate $750 from infrastructure to privacy review and testing, ensuring explicit focus on privacy safeguards and testing in the revised plan. Maintain strict dependence on instructor-approved materials, with robust escalation to human staff for any uncertain or sensitive queries. Monitor, measure, and iterate using the defined success metrics. If progress meets the Expand criteria by Week 12, consider scaled deployment to additional courses; if performance is mixed, apply the Revise/Extend criteria; if critical safety, privacy, or content issues persist, implement Stop criteria and discontinue the pilot.

1. Executive summary and assumptions
- Objective: Launch a pilot AI course-QA assistant to support two undergraduate courses (~300 students) with a part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, and one teaching assistant, within a $5,000 budget.
- Scope: The assistant answers only from instructor-approved course materials, with safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and escalation to human staff. It supports basic student questions, assignment queries, and course logistics; it does not replace instructor or TA functions.
- Assumptions:
  - Instructor-approved materials are provided and maintained in a centralized content repository.
  - Student developers have access to a development environment with sandbox data; privacy controls are enforced.
  - The teaching assistant provides operational support for escalation, student feedback, and monitoring.
  - No new university-wide policies are assumed; approvals are limited to project scope defined here.
  - The contingency in Weeks 6–8 reallocates exactly $750 from infrastructure to privacy review and testing.

2. Goals and non-goals
- Goals:
  - Deliver a reliable 24/7-availability pilot QA assistant for two courses with accurate, cited answers from approved materials.
  - Implement robust safeguards: hallucination detection, privacy protections, academic integrity checks, and clear escalation paths to human staff.
  - Achieve measurable engagement and satisfaction signals from students and instructors.
- Non-goals:
  - Replacing instructors or TAs; not handling admissions, grading, or non-course content.
  - Full university-scale deployment; pilot scope is limited to ~300 students and two courses.
  - Handling non-approved materials or external content without explicit authorization.

3. Stakeholder and decision-rights map
- Stakeholders:
  - Instructor Sponsor (part-time): ultimate content approval, escalation, and pilot success.
  - Student Developers (2): build, test, deploy, monitor the assistant.
  - Teaching Assistant: user support, escalation coordinator, privacy/academic integrity monitor.
  - Course Students (~300): primary users and feedback providers.
  - IT/Privacy Reviewer (external to core team): conducts privacy review and testing during contingency.
- Decision rights:
  - Content approval: Instructor Sponsor.
  - Technical go/no-go: Instructor Sponsor with input from TA.
  - Privacy and security sign-off: Privacy Reviewer.
  - Budget approvals and reallocations: Project lead with instructor sponsor consent.
  - Escalation policy updates: TA lead and Instructor Sponsor.

4. Baseline 12-week roadmap table
- Week 1: Requirements, content inventory, risk register, privacy controls outline, governance plan; set success metrics; establish repository.
- Week 2: Baseline architecture design; data sources mapping; consent and privacy controls; implement content-channel interfaces; start training on approved materials.
- Week 3: Prototype QA flow; hallucination mitigation mechanisms; escalation workflow; initial privacy-by-design checks; create test corpus.
- Week 4: Developer integration sprints; UI/UX for student interactions; implement logging for auditability; establish content approval gate.
- Week 5: End-to-end testing with instructor-supplied material; pilot run with internal testers; prepare risk/mitigation updates; finalize budget plan for initial phase.
- Week 6: Contingency start (no change yet) – continue development; policy for escalation refined; performance testing; begin privacy review readiness; monitoring dashboards.
- Week 7: Student-facing pilot beta in controlled subset; real-time support channel; monitor privacy and integrity controls; gather qualitative feedback.
- Week 8: Contingency continues – infrastructure reallocation decision effective; privacy review/testing intensifies; adjust scope as needed; incident playbooks tested.
- Week 9: Full piloting across both courses with monitoring; bug triage; escalate to human staff as per policy; collect metrics.
- Week 10: Stability phase; refine safeguards; finalize documentation; prepare mid-pilot evaluation report.
- Week 11: Data cleaning and analysis; iterate improvements based on findings; prepare expansion criteria.
- Week 12: Final evaluation; compile success metrics, lessons learned, and readiness for broader deployment; governance sign-off.

5. Baseline resource and budget allocation
- Team roles and ownership:
  - Part-time Faculty Sponsor: content approval, escalation authority, final sign-off on release.
  - 2 Student Developers: lead build, integration, testing, and iterative improvements.
  - Teaching Assistant: monitoring, user support, escalation liaison, privacy/academic integrity checks.
- Baseline budget (TOTAL: $5,000):
  - Infrastructure (hosting, tools, limited compute): $2,000
  - Content development and alignment (content curation tools, licenses if needed): $500
  - Privacy and security testing (audits, privacy tooling, third-party reviews): $1,000
  - QA and testing (test data preparation, test cases, automation): $700
  - Escalation and support operations (training, documentation, help channel): $400
  - Contingency reserve (unused/unspent): $400
- Note: All allocations assume in-kind contributions where possible (student labor treated as part of project work, not monetary).

6. Week 6-8 contingency overlay and ownership changes
- Contingency event: One student developer becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8.
- Ownership changes:
  - Remaining student developer: assumes sole development capacity for features previously split and will absorb additional workload; responsibilities redistributed to maintain velocity.
  - Teaching Assistant: increases involvement in monitoring, escalation handling, and privacy/academic integrity checks; supports knowledge transfer to the remaining developer.
  - Privacy review and testing: intensified focus; part of reallocation plan, with possible external reviewer involvement if workload exceeds capacity.
- Scope adjustments:
  - Scope remains within pilot limits; any new features considered only if they do not exceed budget or risk timeline.
  Changes in milestones:
  - Week 6: Begin contingency execution; re-prioritize backlog to essential safeguards and core QA capabilities.
  - Week 7: Accelerated privacy testing and real-world escalation drills; ensure logs and audit trails are robust.
  - Week 8: Finalize contingency risk mitigation, confirm remaining resource allocations, and prepare for stable rollout.
- Dependencies:
  - Availability of institutional privacy review resources during Weeks 6–8.
  - Clear escalation protocol to human staff with response time targets.
- Testing and support:
  - Increase reliance on automated tests, canned scenarios, and manual escalation drills.
  - Ensure operational hours and channels for student queries remain accessible.

7. Revised budget showing the exact $750 reallocation
- Original dedicated allocation for infrastructure: $2,000 (as baseline).
- Reallocation: $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy review and testing.
- New budget allocations (post-reallocation):
  - Infrastructure: $1,250
  - Privacy and security testing: $1,750 (increase by $750)
  - Content development/licensing: $500
  - QA/testing activities: $700
  - Escalation/support operations: $400
  - Contingency reserve: $400
- Total remains: $5,000
- Impact:
  - More robust privacy review and testing capacity during Weeks 6–8, supporting the contingency and enhancing safeguards without exceeding total budget.

8. Risk register and mitigations
- Risk: Developer unavailability jeopardizes delivery velocity.
  Mitigation: Reassign tasks to remaining developer; adjust backlog; bring TA to assist; document dependencies.
- Risk: Hallucination or incorrect content responses.
  Mitigation: enforce strict source-from-instructor materials; implement citation mechanism; enable escalation to human staff for uncertain responses.
- Risk: Privacy breach or data leakage.
  Mitigation: privacy-by-design controls; redact internal data; audit logs; third-party privacy review during Weeks 6–8.
- Risk: Academic integrity violations (e.g., misuse of the assistant for assignments).
  Mitigation: restrict to course-approved materials; monitoring; escalation to instructor/TA for flagged queries.
- Risk: System outages or performance issues under load.
  Mitigation: scalable infrastructure; rate limiting; offline fallback; monitoring dashboards.
- Risk: Insufficient testing coverage due to contingency.
  Mitigation: prioritize critical paths; leverage automated tests; increase manual review during escalations.
- Risk: Misalignment with instructor expectations.
  Mitigation: frequent reviews with sponsor; gating on content approval before release.

9. Success metrics and data-collection plan
- Quantitative metrics:
  - Answer accuracy against approved material: target ≥ 85% correct matches with citations.
  - Escalation rate: percentage of queries escalated to human staff ≤ 10%.
  - Hallucination rate: incidents of non-contextual or irrelevant answers ≤ 5 per 1,000 queries.
  - Student engagement: number of unique students interacting with the assistant; session duration.
  - Support load: average time to resolve escalations.
  - Privacy/compliance incidents: zero violations; audit findings closed.
- Data collection:
  - Logging of all interactions with timestamps, sources, confidence scores, and citations.
  - Feedback collection via quick surveys after sessions and on-demand.
  - Regular privacy and content review by instructor and TA; incident reports for escalations.
- Review cadence:
  - Weekly dashboards during Weeks 1–12.
  - Mid-pilot evaluation at Week 6–8; final evaluation Week 12.

10. Governance, content approval, and escalation process
- Content governance:
  - All responses must derive solely from instructor-approved course materials; no external content allowed.
  - A citation mechanism is required for each answer referencing the approved material.
- Escalation process:
  - If the assistant cannot confidently answer or detects potential risk (privacy, academic integrity, or policy concern), it escalates to the Teaching Assistant, who then routes to the Instructor Sponsor as needed.
  - Escalation SLAs: initial human review within 1 hour during peak hours; decision and handoff within 2 hours.
- Escalation channels:
  - Dedicated help channel (e.g., a ticketing system or chat queue) for students.
  - Direct escalation path to instructor sponsor for content changes or policy exceptions.
- Content approval workflow:
  - Instructor Sponsor reviews and approves new or updated content blocks before deployment.
  - Any modifications to safeguards require approval updates and re-sign-off.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop decision criteria
- Expand criteria (post Week 12):
  - Expand to additional courses or cohorts if:
    - Target metrics achieved: accuracy ≥ 85%, escalation ≤ 10%, privacy incidents = 0.
    - Satisfaction scores ≥ 4.0/5 in student surveys.
    - Resource utilization within budget; no critical technical debt.
- Revise/Extend criteria:
  - If performance metrics are near thresholds but not quite, implement targeted improvements (e.g., better content tagging, improved citations) and extend the pilot with clear milestones.
- Stop criteria:
  - If any of the following occur:
    - Repeated privacy or academic integrity breaches despite mitigations.
    - Inability to maintain accuracy with approved materials exceeding a pre-defined threshold.
    - Budget overruns beyond $5,000 with no feasible mitigation.
    - Insufficient student engagement to justify continuation.
  - In such cases, pilot scope is halted, and a formal debrief with stakeholders is required.

12. Final recommendation
- Proceed with the baseline plan as described, incorporating the Week 6–8 contingency overlay. The $750 reallocation to privacy review and testing strengthens safeguards during the most uncertain phase and accommodates the reduced personnel capacity. Maintain strict adherence to instructor-approved content, with robust escalation pathways to human staff and clear privacy/academic integrity controls. Monitor key metrics weekly; if success criteria are met by Week 12, consider expansion to additional courses or cohorts within the same budget framework, following the defined governance and approval process. If any stop criteria are triggered, pause deployment, conduct a rapid risk reassessment, and document lessons learned for future iterations.

1. Executive summary and assumptions
- Objective: Launch a 12-week pilot AI course-QA assistant that supports two undergraduate courses (~300 students) with a part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a maximum $5,000 pilot budget.

  - The teaching assistant supports user testing, feedback collection, and escalation workflows.
  - Infrastructure budget includes hosting, data handling, testing environments, and basic monitoring.
  - Stakeholders will participate in weekly review meetings and provide timely feedback.
- Outcome: A controllable, auditable pilot with clear escalation, privacy safeguards, and measurable success criteria.

2. Goals and non-goals
- Goals:
  - Provide accurate, material-bound QA assistance for two undergraduate courses for 300 students.
  - Enforce strict content boundaries: no external data beyond approved materials; transparent escalation to human staff.

  - Full-scale university-wide deployment.
  - Replacement of instructors or TAs; this is a support tool.
  - Guarantee of zero errors; focus on governance, monitoring, and rapid remediation.

3. Stakeholder and decision-rights map
- Primary sponsor: Part-time faculty sponsor
  - Decision rights: Course material approvals, escalation triggers, final acceptance of milestones.
- Product/Delivery Lead (Student Developer 1)
  - Decision rights: Technical architecture, integration, deploy/test plan, budget tracking.
- Content Lead (Student Developer 2)
  - Decision rights: Material curation, answer templates, safety checks, hallucination guards, privacy flows.
- Quality Assurance / Testing TA
  - Decision rights: Test plans, issue triage, user feedback incorporation, bug verification.
- End-user representatives (Course instructors, student reps)
  - Decision rights: Feedback priority, user acceptance criteria.
- Finance/Admin liaison
  - Decision rights: Budget reconciliation, approvals for reallocations, expense tracking.

4. Baseline 12-week roadmap table
- Week 1: Kickoff, requirements alignment, gather instructor-approved materials, establish governance artifacts, set up environments, baseline privacy controls.
- Week 2: Prototyping core QA flows using a narrow syllabus slice; define hallucination detectors; initial escalation workflow draft.
- Week 3: Implement material-bound response logic; initial privacy safeguards; UI/UX design for instructor and TA review.
- Week 4: Internal testing with sample student queries; refine safety prompts and citation behavior; establish logging/monitoring.
- Week 5: End-to-end pilot readiness; user testing with a small student cohort; finalize escalation paths; performance metrics baseline.
- Week 6: Contingency window start (Team note: see Week 6-8 overlay). Core feature stabilization; begin privacy-focused testing; prepare contingency plan artifacts.
- Week 7: Deploy to broader student group in a controlled manner; monitor for hallucinations, privacy events; collect feedback.
- Week 8: Contingency execution continues; infrastructure budget reallocation begins (per override); privacy review/testing intensifies.
- Week 9: Refinement based on feedback; prepare documentation for stakeholders; begin formal testing report drafting.
- Week 10: Finalizing content approvals; run regression tests; ensure escalation and human-in-the-loop readiness.
- Week 11: Pre-launch readiness review; compile metrics, risks, and mitigations; finalize support handoff to staff.

  - Teaching Assistant: 6–8 hours/week for testing, feedback collection, and escalation handling.
- Infrastructure and tools (budget line items):
  - Hosting/compute for pilot: 40–60% of budget.
  - Data handling, privacy tooling, logging: 20–30%.
  - Testing environments, QA automation, monitoring: 10–20%.
  - Contingency reserve for unplanned needs: 5–10%.

- Note: Exact allocations to be detailed in section 5 and will be revised in section 7 to reflect the contingency.

- Trigger: One student developer becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8.

  - Reallocate Week 6-8 workload: Student Developer 2 (content and safety) absorbs additional responsibilities for architectural stability and testing, while Student Developer 1’s remaining tasks are redistributed to TA and Developer 2 as feasible.

  - Increased reliance on instructor sponsor for approval gating and escalation monitoring; TA supports intensified testing and feedback collection.
- Plan changes:
  - Scope reduction: Freeze non-critical features; prioritize privacy, escalation, and safety controls; ensure material-bound correctness.
  - Dependencies: Ensure documentation exists for handoffs and knowledge transfer; maintain test cases that can be run with reduced developer bandwidth.
  - Testing: Accelerate privacy audits, data-flow verification, and human-in-the-loop escalation drills.
  - Support: Establish a capped escalation queue and SLA with human staff for Week 6-8 and beyond.
- Milestones impacted:
  - Week 6: Stabilize core safety and material-bound responses; confirm fallback paths when a developer is unavailable.
  - Week 7-8: Privacy review deepening and testing; complete reallocation workload and maintain pilot integrity.

7. Revised budget showing the exact $750 reallocation

  - Privacy tooling and security testing: $900
  - Data management and logging: $700

    - Privacy tooling and security testing: $1,150 (increased by $450)
    - Data management and logging: $700
    - QA testing and coverage: $900
    - Coordination, misc, and contingency: $450 (reduced by $250)

8. Risk register and mitigations
- Risk: Hallucination or non-material-based responses.
  - Mitigation: Enforce material-bound responses; implement robust citation and fallback to human escalation.
- Risk: Privacy breach or data leakage.
  - Mitigation: Data minimization; access controls; audit logging; privacy review drill; encryption at rest/in transit.

  - Mitigation: Strict reference policies; blocking external data sources; escalation to staff for suspected instances.

- Risk: Budget overruns.
  - Mitigation: Tight scope for Weeks 6-12; explicit contingency spending controls; weekly budget reviews.
- Risk: Inadequate instructor engagement or feedback loop.
  - Mitigation: Scheduled weekly check-ins; clear escalation queues; TA-led user feedback sessions.
- Risk: System performance issues with 300 students.
  - Mitigation: Incremental rollout; load-testing; monitoring dashboards.

9. Success metrics and data-collection plan
- Quantitative:
  - Accuracy rate of AI-provided answers using approved materials (target ≥ 85% in Week 12 pilot tests).
  - Escalation rate to human staff (target ≤ 10% of interactions; monitor spikes).
  - Number of privacy incidents (target 0).
  - Time-to-first-escalation (target < 5 minutes for urgent edge cases).
  - User satisfaction score (CSAT) from student/TA/instructor feedback (target ≥ 4.0/5).
- Qualitative:
  - Content reviews of a sample of answered questions for alignment with materials.
  - Feedback on clarity and usefulness of citations.
  - Observations on whether students rely on the AI in ways that support learning outcomes.
- Data collection:
  - Logging of all interactions (anonymized as appropriate).
  - Regular privacy/audit reviews by designated staff.
  - Weekly surveys for instructors and TAs; post-week 12 final survey.

10. Governance, content approval, and escalation process
- Content approval:
  - All AI responses must be constrained to instructor-approved materials; any inference beyond that triggers escalation.
  - A living content policy document detailing allowed/blocked content, citation requirements, and update cadence.
- Escalation process:
  - Tier 1: Student question handled by AI with citation; if uncertain or policy breach detected, escalate to Teaching Assistant.
  - Tier 2: If TA flags issue, escalate to Part-time Faculty Sponsor for review and possible instructor override.
  - Tier 3: For sensitive or policy-related concerns, escalate to designated staff for direct human support.
- Privacy safeguards:
  - Data minimization: Only necessary student data is captured; no PII exposure beyond required.
  - Access controls: Role-based access to logs and content repositories.
  - Audit logs: Immutable logs for all interactions and escalations.
- Governance cadence:
  - Weekly governance review meetings with sponsor, developers, TA, and data/privacy liaison.
  - Monthly content review and update cycle with instructors.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop decision criteria
- Expand criteria (post Week 12):
  - Expand to additional courses and up to 1,000 students with proven safety and efficacy; implement scaled infrastructure.
  - Criteria: Sustained accuracy ≥ 85% across courses, escalation rate ≤ 10%, zero privacy incidents, documented savings in TA time, positive user feedback.
- Revise/Extend criteria:
  - If accuracy or safety metrics are lower than targets (e.g., sustained 75–84%), extend pilot with additional content validations, more human-in-the-loop, and adjusted scopes; document required resources.
  - If user satisfaction is moderate but improvement opportunities exist, extend by additional 2–4 weeks for iteration cycles and content refinement.
- Stop criteria:
  - Recurrent privacy incidents with unresolved remediation; failure to meet safety or escalation SLAs in two consecutive weeks; unsustainable budget pressure; inability to roll out to additional courses due to governance; critical operational failures that reduce trust or violate student rights.

12. Final recommendation
- Proceed with the baseline 12-week launch as described, with the Week 6-8 contingency overlay activated if staffing constraints occur. The $750 reallocation to privacy review and testing is prudent to reinforce safeguards and align with the pilot’s privacy and integrity requirements. Maintain strict material-bound operation, robust escalation paths, and ongoing measurement of accuracy, escalation rate, and privacy incidents. If Week 12 metrics meet the Expand criteria, plan for scaled deployment to additional courses and/or larger cohorts; if metrics fall short, implement the Revise/Extend plan, tighten scope, and consider stopping escalation points if safety cannot be assured. Ensure weekly governance cadence and transparent reporting to stakeholders. Assumptions: instructor-approved materials are accessible, staff availability for escalation remains sufficient, and the budget remains within $5,000.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Executive summary and assumptions
- Objective: Pilot an AI course-QA assistant to support two undergraduate courses (~300 students) with mentor guidance from one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, and one teaching assistant. Budget: up to $5,000 for 12 weeks.
- Scope: The assistant may answer only from instructor-approved course materials. Safeguards required: hallucination mitigation, privacy protection, academic integrity, and escalation to human staff.
- Assumptions:
  - Instructor-approved materials exist and are codified in a central repository accessible to the AI.
  - Student developers have basic ML/AI and software development skills; TA can assist in QA and student-facing support.
  - Infrastructure needs are modest (hosting, logging, basic privacy controls). No external policy changes are assumed.
  - No university-wide approvals assumed beyond internal project approvals; all work complies with applicable privacy and academic integrity standards.
- Deliverables focus on a practical 12-week rollout with a defined contingency impacting Weeks 6–8.

2. Goals and non-goals
- Goals:
  - Deliver a compliant, instructor-approved QA assistant that supports course materials with safe behavior.
  - Establish clear escalation paths to human staff.
  - Implement safeguards for hallucinations, privacy, and academic integrity.
  - Achieve stable operation for both courses with ~300 students.
  - Complete a post-Week-12 evaluation with actionable recommendations.
- Non-goals:
  - Replacing instructors or TAs.
  - Full-scale university-wide deployment beyond the pilot scope.
  - Guarantee of perfect accuracy or zero failure modes.

3. Stakeholder and decision-rights map
- Primary stakeholders: Part-time faculty sponsor (content oversight, escalation authority), two student developers (implementation, maintenance), one teaching assistant (student support, QA, monitoring), students (end users), and pilot governance/IT liaison (budget, policy alignment).
- Decision-rights:
  - Content approvals: Faculty sponsor.
  - Feature scope and milestones: Project lead (one of the student developers with oversight).
  - Budget commits and reallocations: Authorized within $5,000 total by project lead with sponsor sign-off.
  - Escalation policy and privacy/compliance: Faculty sponsor and IT/privacy liaison.
  - Go/No-Go for milestones: Steering review at Week 5 and Week 12.

4. Baseline 12-week roadmap table
- Week 0–2: Planning and material alignment
  - Define approved problem scope, data sources, and privacy controls.
  - Set up development environment, basic CI/CD, and version control.
  - Create escalation and logging processes.
- Week 3–4: Core MVP development
  - Implement QA assistant skeleton capable of answering from approved materials.
  - Implement hallucination checks (source attribution, confidence scoring).
  - Implement privacy safeguards (data minimization, no PII storage, audit trails).
- Week 5: Interim review and readiness gate
  - Demonstrate MVP with sandbox data; validate safety controls; prepare for pilot rollout.
  - Align on measurement plan and success criteria.
- Week 6–7: Pilot deployment and monitoring
  - Roll out to a limited student subset; monitor interactions, logs, and escalations.
  - Refine prompts, safety policies, and moderation rules.
- Week 8: Contingency overlay begins (see next section)
- Week 9–10: Full pilot iteration
  - Expand access to remaining students; tighten privacy review and testing routines.
- Week 11: Evaluation prep
  - Collect metrics, feedback, and prepare final report.
- Week 12: Final review and decision gate
  - Assess Expand/Revise/Stop criteria; determine next steps.

5. Baseline resource and budget allocation
- Roles and allocations (baseline):
  - Part-time faculty sponsor: 10–15 hours total across weeks 0–12 for oversight and approvals.
  - Two student developers: 40 hours/week total combined (~480 hours across 12 weeks) for design, implementation, and maintenance.
  - Teaching assistant: 20 hours/week total (~240 hours) for QA, student support, and monitoring.
  - Infrastructure/hosting: Allocated budget portion (initial): $1,250–$1,600 for hosting, logging, basic security, and tooling.
  - Content curation and QA tooling: $800–$1,000 for licenses or services (if needed).
  - Contingency and testing buffer: $400–$600 reserved for unplanned tests or minor tooling.
  - Total baseline budget: $4,000–$5,000.
- Assumptions: No external expenditures beyond the stated budget; all tools and services chosen are budget-conscious and compliant with requirements.

6. Week 6-8 contingency overlay and ownership changes
- Contingency scenario: One student developer becomes unavailable during Weeks 6–8. Exactly $750 originally allocated to infrastructure must be reallocated to privacy review and testing.
- Changes to scope and ownership:
  - Ownership shift: Remaining student developer(s) assume primary implementation tasks; Teaching Assistant and sponsor provide increased QA, risk monitoring, and escalation readiness.
  - Infrastructure reduction: Infrastructure budget is reduced by $750 for Weeks 6–8; the remaining infrastructure budget (if any) must support essential hosting and logging for the reduced team, or be reallocated elsewhere.
  - Privacy review and testing emphasis: $750 is reallocated to privacy review and testing activities, including security checks, data handling reviews, and privacy-by-design verification.
  - Dependencies and milestones affected:
    - MVP stability and reliability become higher priority; additional manual testing cycles required.
    - Escalation pathways formalized and stress-tested.
  - Testing plan adjustments:
    - Increase manual QA and peer reviews.
    - Implement automated privacy checks within available tooling, if feasible within the reduced budget.
  - Risks increased:
    - Reduced manpower may affect feature completeness and response quality; mitigated by prioritizing critical features and relying on sponsor/TA for governance.

7. Revised budget showing the exact $750 reallocation
Baseline budget components (illustrative with numbers):
- Hosting and infrastructure: $1,600
- Content curation/tools: $1,000
- QA tooling and testing: $700
- Staffing (sponsor, TAs, developers): $0 (in-kind time within project)
- Miscellaneous/contingency: $700
- Total baseline: $4,000
Contingency overlay (Week 6–8):
- Reallocate $750 from Infrastructure to Privacy Review and Testing:
  - New infrastructure budget: $1,600 − $750 = $850
  - Privacy review and testing budget: $0 + $750 = $750
- Adjusted totals:
  - Hosting/Infrastructure: $850
  - Privacy Review/Testing: $750
  - Content/tools: $1,000
  - Misc/contingency: $700
  - Staffing (in-kind): remains $0 to explicit budgets
  - Total revised budget: $3,300
Note: The total budget cannot exceed $5,000; this revision keeps within limit. If additional hidden costs arise, they must be approved by sponsor.

8. Risk register and mitigations
- Risk: Developer unavailability during Weeks 6–8
  - Mitigation: Cross-train TA/backup developers; predefine critical path tasks; implement lightweight task board with clear ownership.
- Risk: Hallucination remains high
  - Mitigation: Strict source attribution, confidence thresholds, mandatory escalation for low-confidence responses; implement a “quote from source” mechanism.
- Risk: Privacy breaches or PII collection
  - Mitigation: Data minimization, never store student PII; logging scrub; privacy-by-design review; alignment with sponsor.
- Risk: Academic integrity violations
  - Mitigation: Use only approved materials; add explicit disclaimers; route questionable queries to human staff.
- Risk: Escalation overload
  - Mitigation: Clear escalation tiers; set response SLAs; ensure human staff overlap during peak times.
- Risk: Budget overrun
  - Mitigation: Regular budget tracking; revert non-critical features if required.

9. Success metrics and data-collection plan
- Quantitative metrics:
  - Coverage: Percentage of instructor-approved materials successfully answerable by AI.
  - Safety: Percentage of interactions that trigger safeguards (PII redaction, escalation, low-confidence refusals).
  - Accuracy: Alignment of AI responses with instructor-approved materials (via expert audit).
  - Escalation rate: Proportion of queries escalated to human staff.
  - Response latency: Average time to first response.
  - Student satisfaction: End-of-pilot survey rating.
- Data collection methods:
  - Logging of all interactions with anonymized identifiers.
  - Regular content audits comparing AI responses to approved sources.
  - Escalation logs with reason codes.
  - Surveys at Week 6 (mid-pilot) and Week 12 (end-pilot).
- Targets (illustrative):
  - 95% of answered queries drawn from approved materials.
  - <5% escalation rate for safe, well-supported questions; higher only for edge cases with escalation.
  - Average response <2 seconds for standard queries; under 60 seconds for escalations.
  - Privacy incidents: 0; audits show no PII leakage.
  - Satisfaction: average rating >=4.0/5.

10. Governance, content approval, and escalation process
- Content governance:
  - All AI responses must be constrained to instructor-approved materials only.
  - Hallucination handling: If the system cannot locate a response in approved sources, respond with a safe refusal and offer to escalate.
- Escalation process:
  - Tier 1: Immediate escalation to TA for ambiguous queries or potential integrity concerns.
  - Tier 2: Escalation to instructor sponsor for content-sensitive issues or policy questions.
  - Tier 3: Human staff intervention for privacy, safety, or high-risk cases.
- Privacy controls:
  - No collection of identifiable student data beyond what is necessary for session context; ensure data minimization and data retention policies.
  - PII sanitization in logs; access restricted to authorized personnel.
- Quality assurance:
  - Weekly review of a sample of interactions by the sponsor and TA.
  - Retrospectives to update safeguards, prompts, and allowed sources.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop decision criteria
- Expand (Scale up next cycle if):
  - Baseline targets met or exceeded (accuracy, safety, user satisfaction > target thresholds).
  - Privacy and academic integrity controls proven effective with low escalation rates.
  - Additional resources are available to broaden course coverage or add new features (e.g., expanded subject areas).
- Revise/Extend (Modify with improvements):
  - If certain modules underperform (e.g., some topics with higher hallucination risk), refine prompts, tooling, or data sources; extend testing windows; adjust expectations.
- Stop (Terminate pilot):
  - If frequent violations of privacy, persistent high-risk escalations, or significant inability to operate within budget, or if no measurable benefit is observed after Week 12.

12. Final recommendation
- Proceed with the baseline plan, executing the Week 6–8 contingency as described, with the exact $750 reallocation to privacy review and testing. Maintain tight governance and escalation processes, ensuring all interactions stay within instructor-approved materials and privacy standards. Monitor key metrics closely, and be prepared to Shift to “Revise/Extend” or “Stop” based on Week 12 outcomes. The pilot should deliver a functional, safe, and instructor-aligned AI QA assistant for two undergraduate courses, with clear escalation pathways and measurable outcomes within the $5,000 ceiling.

  Changes in milestones:
  - Week 6: Begin contingency execution; re-prioritize backlog to essential safeguards and core QA capabilities.
  - Week 7: Accelerated privacy testing and real-world escalation drills; ensure logs and audit trails are robust.
  - Week 8: Finalize contingency risk mitigation, confirm remaining resource allocations, and prepare for stable rollout.
- Dependencies:
  - Availability of institutional privacy review resources during Weeks 6–8.
  - Clear escalation protocol to human staff with response time targets.
- Testing and support:
  - Increase reliance on automated tests, canned scenarios, and manual escalation drills.
  - Ensure operational hours and channels for student queries remain accessible.

7. Revised budget showing the exact $750 reallocation
- Original dedicated allocation for infrastructure: $2,000 (as baseline).
- Reallocation: $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy review and testing.
- New budget allocations (post-reallocation):
  - Infrastructure: $1,250
  - Privacy and security testing: $1,750 (increase by $750)
  - Content development/licensing: $500
  - QA/testing activities: $700
  - Escalation/support operations: $400
  - Contingency reserve: $400
- Total remains: $5,000
- Impact:
  - More robust privacy review and testing capacity during Weeks 6–8, supporting the contingency and enhancing safeguards without exceeding total budget.

8. Risk register and mitigations
- Risk: Developer unavailability jeopardizes delivery velocity.
  Mitigation: Reassign tasks to remaining developer; adjust backlog; bring TA to assist; document dependencies.
- Risk: Hallucination or incorrect content responses.
  Mitigation: enforce strict source-from-instructor materials; implement citation mechanism; enable escalation to human staff for uncertain responses.
- Risk: Privacy breach or data leakage.
  Mitigation: privacy-by-design controls; redact internal data; audit logs; third-party privacy review during Weeks 6–8.
- Risk: Academic integrity violations (e.g., misuse of the assistant for assignments).
  Mitigation: restrict to course-approved materials; monitoring; escalation to instructor/TA for flagged queries.
- Risk: System outages or performance issues under load.
  Mitigation: scalable infrastructure; rate limiting; offline fallback; monitoring dashboards.
- Risk: Insufficient testing coverage due to contingency.
  Mitigation: prioritize critical paths; leverage automated tests; increase manual review during escalations.
- Risk: Misalignment with instructor expectations.
  Mitigation: frequent reviews with sponsor; gating on content approval before release.

9. Success metrics and data-collection plan
- Quantitative metrics:
  - Answer accuracy against approved material: target ≥ 85% correct matches with citations.
  - Escalation rate: percentage of queries escalated to human staff ≤ 10%.
  - Hallucination rate: incidents of non-contextual or irrelevant answers ≤ 5 per 1,000 queries.
  - Student engagement: number of unique students interacting with the assistant; session duration.
  - Support load: average time to resolve escalations.
  - Privacy/compliance incidents: zero violations; audit findings closed.
- Data collection:
  - Logging of all interactions with timestamps, sources, confidence scores, and citations.
  - Feedback collection via quick surveys after sessions and on-demand.
  - Regular privacy and content review by instructor and TA; incident reports for escalations.
- Review cadence:
  - Weekly dashboards during Weeks 1–12.
  - Mid-pilot evaluation at Week 6–8; final evaluation Week 12.

10. Governance, content approval, and escalation process
- Content governance:
  - All responses must derive solely from instructor-approved course materials; no external content allowed.
  - A citation mechanism is required for each answer referencing the approved material.
- Escalation process:
  - If the assistant cannot confidently answer or detects potential risk (privacy, academic integrity, or policy concern), it escalates to the Teaching Assistant, who then routes to the Instructor Sponsor as needed.
  - Escalation SLAs: initial human review within 1 hour during peak hours; decision and handoff within 2 hours.
- Escalation channels:
  - Dedicated help channel (e.g., a ticketing system or chat queue) for students.
  - Direct escalation path to instructor sponsor for content changes or policy exceptions.
- Content approval workflow:
  - Instructor Sponsor reviews and approves new or updated content blocks before deployment.
  - Any modifications to safeguards require approval updates and re-sign-off.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop decision criteria
- Expand criteria (post Week 12):
  - Expand to additional courses or cohorts if:
    - Target metrics achieved: accuracy ≥ 85%, escalation ≤ 10%, privacy incidents = 0.
    - Satisfaction scores ≥ 4.0/5 in student surveys.
    - Resource utilization within budget; no critical technical debt.
- Revise/Extend criteria:
  - If performance metrics are near thresholds but not quite, implement targeted improvements (e.g., better content tagging, improved citations) and extend the pilot with clear milestones.
- Stop criteria:
  - If any of the following occur:
    - Repeated privacy or academic integrity breaches despite mitigations.
    - Inability to maintain accuracy with approved materials exceeding a pre-defined threshold.
    - Budget overruns beyond $5,000 with no feasible mitigation.
    - Insufficient student engagement to justify continuation.
  - In such cases, pilot scope is halted, and a formal debrief with stakeholders is required.

12. Final recommendation
- Proceed with the baseline plan as described, incorporating the Week 6–8 contingency overlay. The $750 reallocation to privacy review and testing strengthens safeguards during the most uncertain phase and accommodates the reduced personnel capacity. Maintain strict adherence to instructor-approved content, with robust escalation pathways to human staff and clear privacy/academic integrity controls. Monitor key metrics weekly; if success criteria are met by Week 12, consider expansion to additional courses or cohorts within the same budget framework, following the defined governance and approval process. If any stop criteria are triggered, pause deployment, conduct a rapid risk reassessment, and document lessons learned for future iterations.

agent_task.prompt:
Create a 12-week strategic launch plan for a pilot AI course-QA assistant supporting two undergraduate courses and about 300 students. The team has one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a maximum $5,000 pilot budget. The assistant may answer only from instructor-approved course materials and must include safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and escalation to human staff.

Build the baseline plan first. Then apply this required contingency at the end of Week 5: one student developer becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8, and exactly $750 originally allocated to infrastructure must be reallocated to privacy review and testing. The total budget may not exceed $5,000. Show how scope, ownership, dependencies, testing, support, milestones, and decision gates change; do not merely shift dates.

Define measurable Expand, Revise/Extend, and Stop criteria after Week 12. Do not invent university policies, approvals, completed results, or legal guarantees. Label assumptions.

6. Week 6-8 contingency overlay and ownership changes
7. Revised budget showing the exact $750 reallocation
8. Risk register and mitigations
9. Success metrics and data-collection plan
10. Governance, content approval, and escalation process
11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop decision criteria
12. Final recommendation
